title: Fix user story ID collision: FEAT-002 and FEAT-003 both define US-010

title: Add run-as-user user story to FEAT-001 specifying mechanism
acceptance: Specifies privilege-dropping mechanism (su/sudo/setpriv)

acceptance: Strategy covers testing of secret-dependent providers in earlier phases

title: Add explicit test-to-user-story acceptance criteria mapping

acceptance: Secret stub strategy for Phase 1 documented if secrets remain after Phase 1

title: User story ID collision: US-010 in both FEAT-002 and FEAT-003
description: FEAT-002 and FEAT-003 both use US-010 after the recent renumbering, which breaks story traceability across the feature stack.
design: Choose the canonical story number for each feature, renumber the conflicting heading and any local references, and confirm no other feature reuses the same story ID.
acceptance: FEAT-002 and FEAT-003 use distinct story IDs and US-010 appears only once across docs/helix/01-frame/features.
notes: Coverage: implementation-plan Phase 3.

title: PRD P0-14 says pip but should say pipx
description: PRD P0-14 still says pip even though FEAT-003 and SD-001 now use pipx for global Python CLI tooling.
design: Update the PRD wording to pipx and keep it aligned with FEAT-003 and SD-001 run-as-user guidance.
acceptance: PRD P0-14 references pipx instead of pip and matches FEAT-003 US-017 plus SD-001.
notes: Coverage: implementation-plan Phase 4.

title: SD-001 traceability table stale after PRD P0 renumbering
description: SD-001 traceability still reflects old PRD P0 numbering and is missing the current run-as-user and template-variable rows.
design: Rebuild the SD-001 traceability table against the current PRD must-have list so each P0 requirement appears exactly once.
acceptance: SD-001 includes the current P0 numbering with rows for run-as-user, fail-stop, single binary, and built-in template variables.
notes: Artifact reconciliation issue.

title: US-016 (npm) missing run-as-user acceptance criterion
description: FEAT-003 says brew, npm, and pipx must run as an unprivileged user, but US-016 does not encode that for npm packages.
design: Add an explicit run-as-user acceptance bullet to US-016 that matches the brew and pipx stories.
acceptance: US-016 explicitly states that npm operations run as the configured unprivileged user rather than root.
notes: Coverage: implementation-plan Phase 4.

title: FEAT-001 US-005 secret chain order missing pre-resolved position
description: FEAT-001 US-005 still documents only env vars then 1Password and does not place pre-resolved secrets at the head of the provider chain.
design: Rewrite US-005 so the provider order matches SD-001 exactly: pre-resolved file, env vars, 1Password, custom, then fail.
acceptance: FEAT-001 US-005 states the full provider chain and matches the SD-001 secret provider section.
notes: Coverage: implementation-plan Phase 6.

title: Cover Implementation Plan Phase 1 with bounded execution issues
description: Phase 1 needed bounded execution slices for the core engine queue before implementation begins.
design: Decompose Phase 1 into independently verifiable execution issues for CLI, engine, and apply foundations.
acceptance: The tracker contains bounded follow-up issues covering every Phase 1 deliverable cluster and its tests.
notes: Decomposition complete. Child execution slices: hx-8c0acfc4, hx-620c99d7, hx-6382d94b.

title: Cover Implementation Plan Phase 2 file-provider scope with bounded issues
description: Phase 2 needed bounded slices for template_file, static_file, file_copy, file_absent, directory, symlink, and metadata work.
design: Split the Phase 2 provider surface into independently verifiable file-provider slices with golden-plan and mock-system verification hooks.
acceptance: The tracker contains bounded follow-up issues covering the Phase 2 provider families and metadata work.
notes: Decomposition complete. Child execution slices: hx-355a7c95, hx-26051a6c, hx-b4a47d34.

title: Cover Implementation Plan Phase 5 user/service/network scope with bounded issues
description: Phase 5 needed bounded slices for user/access, systemd, Docker, and system utility providers.
design: Decompose by subsystem boundaries so user/access, service management, Docker, and utility primitives can land independently.
acceptance: The tracker contains bounded follow-up issues covering each Phase 5 deliverable cluster.
notes: Decomposition complete. Child execution slices: hx-627c2aad, hx-5da7be80, hx-5f438ba8, hx-125b91ab.

title: Align Implementation Plan Phase 6 secret-management wording to SD-001
description: Implementation Plan Phase 6 still summarizes the secret chain as env vars then 1Password, but SD-001 defines the authoritative order as pre-resolved file, env vars, 1Password, custom, then fail.
design: Update the Phase 6 wording so the implementation plan inherits SD-001 and FEAT-001 without losing the secret-file and masked-output deliverables.
acceptance: Implementation Plan Phase 6 describes the same secret-provider chain as SD-001 and FEAT-001 US-005.

title: Cover Implementation Plan Phase 7 storage/auth scope with bounded issues
description: Phase 7 needed bounded slices for ZFS dataset/property work and Kerberos KDC, principal, and keytab providers.
design: Split Phase 7 into independently verifiable storage and authentication issues with explicit destructive-operation safeguards.
acceptance: The tracker contains bounded follow-up issues covering the ZFS and Kerberos deliverables in Phase 7.
notes: Decomposition complete. Child execution slices: hx-ee1eb54e, hx-fe52f9c5.

title: Phase 8 references missing FEAT-010 governing artifact
description: Implementation Plan Phase 8 is labeled Composites and Polish (FEAT-010), but the repo feature-spec stack only defines FEAT-001 through FEAT-009.
design: Either introduce the missing governing artifact for Phase 8 or retarget the phase to existing artifacts, then update tracker traceability.
acceptance: Phase 8 cites an existing governing artifact in docs/helix and the tracker can reference that artifact consistently for follow-up work.

title: Cover Implementation Plan Phase 9 reference-deployment migration with bounded issues
description: Phase 9 needed bounded follow-up issues for the timbuktu and sahara migrations, side-by-side validation, and migration documentation.
design: Split migration work by deployment target and by validation/documentation concern so the two reference deployments can progress incrementally.
acceptance: The tracker contains bounded follow-up issues covering both reference deployments, side-by-side validation, and required migration documentation.
notes: Decomposition complete. Child execution slices: hx-a84be396, hx-67932b43, hx-f9da44d7.

title: Add tracker coverage for implementation-plan validation milestones
description: The implementation plan defines validation milestones after Phases 1, 3, 5, 7, and 9, and the tracker needed explicit coverage for each milestone evidence gate.
design: Create milestone issues that map each validation row to prerequisite execution slices and the expected evidence in the test plan.
acceptance: The tracker contains a concrete plan for all validation milestones in the implementation plan.
notes: Validation-milestone coverage decomposed into hx-3679cc6b, hx-13c3de94, hx-2eb5dcd2, hx-b497aadf, and hx-74ff8951.

title: Add tracker coverage for implementation-plan validation milestones
description: Duplicate of the canonical validation-milestones issue created during an earlier stale-lock retry.
design: Use hx-2c6ff11d as the canonical issue.
acceptance: Traceability points to hx-2c6ff11d rather than this duplicate.
notes: Duplicate of hx-2c6ff11d created during a stale-lock retry. Use hx-2c6ff11d as the canonical validation-milestones coverage issue.

title: Cover Implementation Plan Phase 3 manifest-composition scope with bounded issues
description: Phase 3 needed queue-shaping coverage for include resolution, variable precedence, iterator expansion, two-pass template evaluation, and notify/trigger behavior.
design: Create bounded build issues that map each Phase 3 deliverable cluster to FEAT-002 and the test plan.
acceptance: The tracker contains bounded Phase 3 follow-up issues covering include resolution, variable precedence, iterator/template expansion, and notify/trigger behavior.
notes: Decomposition complete. Child execution slices: hx-bd58fd0e, hx-dbd88995, hx-f7bd2ed9.

title: Cover Implementation Plan Phase 4 package-provider scope with bounded issues
description: Phase 4 needed bounded build slices for the package-provider surface described in FEAT-003.
design: Split package-provider work by provider family and shared run-as-user behavior so apt, repo/deb, brew, language packages, and alternative distro providers can be implemented independently.
acceptance: The tracker contains bounded Phase 4 follow-up issues covering every provider family in Phase 4 with FEAT-003 traceability.
notes: Decomposition complete. Child execution slices: hx-0ed8ac1f, hx-f6200116, hx-f2212616, hx-fdaf0737, hx-4e3b7202.

title: Cover Implementation Plan Phase 6 secret-management scope with bounded issues
description: Phase 6 needed bounded implementation slices for secret references, runtime resolution, secret files, masked output, and generation behavior.
design: Create build issues that separate secret runtime behavior from secret-file materialization while keeping the governing secret-provider wording authoritative.
acceptance: The tracker contains bounded Phase 6 follow-up issues covering secret resolution and secret-file behavior.
notes: Decomposition complete. Child execution slices: hx-997ed375 and hx-d2050808.

title: Resolve missing governing artifact coverage for NFS and Samba deployment scope
description: The implementation plan and PRD success criteria expect timbuktu validation to cover NFS and Samba, but the current feature stack contains no governing artifact for that scope.
design: Either add governing feature/design artifacts for NFS and Samba provisioning or retarget the downstream milestones to the currently authorized provider set.
acceptance: The artifact stack explicitly accounts for the NFS and Samba scope referenced by the implementation plan and PRD, or those downstream milestones are updated to remove the unsupported scope.

title: Align Implementation Plan Phase 4 package list with FEAT-003 pipx provider naming
description: Implementation Plan Phase 4 still lists pip_packages, but FEAT-003 renamed that provider to python_packages and defined pipx-backed behavior for global Python CLI tools.
design: Update the Phase 4 deliverables so the implementation plan uses the FEAT-003 provider name and pipx wording consistently with the PRD and solution design.
acceptance: Implementation Plan Phase 4 references the current python_packages or pipx-aligned naming rather than pip_packages.

title: Phase 1: implement CLI validate or plan or apply skeleton with manifest loading
description: Implement the top-level Cobra command surface for validate, plan, apply, and version together with manifest loading and validation entrypoints.
design: Keep this slice focused on command dispatch, exit codes, manifest parsing boundaries, and validation-time error reporting.
acceptance: The anneal validate, anneal plan, anneal apply, and anneal version command entrypoints exist with deterministic exit behavior and manifest parsing errors fail during validate without system access.

title: Phase 1: implement variable resolution, DAG construction, and plan assembly
description: Implement variable resolution, template-variable injection, resource dependency graph construction, cycle detection, provider ordering, and plan script assembly.
design: Build the core engine around the SD-001 config-loader and engine contracts so plan generation can be verified before apply execution is introduced.
acceptance: Variable resolution and built-in template variables follow the documented precedence rules, DAG sorting handles cycles and diamonds deterministically, and plan generation emits readable stdlib operations in dependency order.

title: Phase 1: implement apply execution, System abstraction, and inline file provider proof
description: Implement the System interface, shell-backed plan execution, re-plan and compare validation during apply, fail-stop summaries, and one inline file provider that proves the pipeline end to end.
design: Land the minimal executable core in one slice so Phase 1 ends with a real validate/plan/apply loop and the mock-system seam needed for later provider work.
acceptance: Saved-plan apply aborts on drift, fail-stop summaries identify applied, failed, and skipped resources, and a minimal file resource converges end to end through read, diff, emit, and execute.

title: Phase 2: implement template_file, static_file, and file_copy providers
description: Implement the file-provider slice that materializes rendered templates, verbatim static files, and managed path-to-path copies using the Phase 1 file pipeline.
design: Keep these file-writing providers together because they share content rendering, checksum or diff behavior, and golden-plan verification patterns.
acceptance: template_file, static_file, and file_copy each read current state, emit the expected stdlib file operations, and produce no operations when the destination already matches the declared content.

title: Phase 2: implement directory, symlink, and file_absent providers
description: Implement the path-shaping providers that create directories, manage symlink targets, and remove files or glob-matched paths.
design: Group the path lifecycle providers together because they share filesystem-state reads and converge through mkdir, symlink, and remove operations rather than content rendering.
acceptance: directory, symlink, and file_absent converge idempotently for present and absent cases, including target changes and already-correct state.

title: Phase 2: implement mode or ownership enforcement and file-plan diff coverage
description: Add file metadata enforcement for mode and ownership and ensure plan output shows meaningful content or metadata diffs for file-oriented providers.
design: Treat metadata and diff rendering as one cross-cutting slice over the Phase 1 and Phase 2 file providers.
acceptance: Managed file resources emit chmod or chown operations only when metadata differs, and plan output shows the intended content or metadata diff without exposing secrets.

title: Phase 3: implement include graph resolution and variable precedence
description: Implement module include resolution with cycle detection and the documented precedence chain of module defaults, root vars, host file overrides, and environment overrides.
design: Keep include graphing and precedence together because they define the resolved manifest contract that later iterator and trigger work consumes.
acceptance: Included manifests resolve deterministically, include cycles fail validation with the cycle path, and variable precedence matches FEAT-002 and SD-001.

title: Phase 3: implement iterator expansion and two-pass template evaluation
description: Implement each expansion and the two-pass template evaluation flow required to expand iterator data before final variable interpolation.
design: Land iterator handling separately from basic includes so template expansion bugs stay bounded to the config-loader pass structure documented in SD-001.
acceptance: Iterator resources expand into deterministic concrete resources, pass-one expansion happens before pass-two interpolation, and template expressions can use the documented built-in variables.

title: Phase 3: implement notify or trigger ordering and changed-only firing
description: Implement notify edges and trigger resources so dependent restart or command actions fire only when the notifying resource actually changes.
design: Keep trigger behavior separate from config expansion so changed-state tracking and post-resource plan emission can be verified as an engine concern.
acceptance: Trigger resources are ordered after their notifying resources, unchanged resources do not fire triggers, and changed resources do.

title: Phase 4: implement apt_packages, apt_purge, and debconf preseeding
description: Implement the Debian package-management slice covering installation, purge, and optional debconf preseeding before package operations.
design: Keep apt install, purge, and debconf together because they share dpkg or apt state reads and are validated in the same Ubuntu integration environment.
acceptance: apt_packages installs missing packages in batch, apt_purge removes installed packages, debconf values are applied before install when declared, and converged package sets produce no operations.

title: Phase 4: implement apt_repo and deb_install providers
description: Implement external apt repository management and vendor deb package installation with version tracking and repository-before-package ordering.
design: Land repository and deb-file support together because both extend the Debian package surface but have separate state tracking from standard apt package lists.
acceptance: apt_repo manages signing keys and sources entries idempotently, resources depending on a repo sort after it, and deb_install reacts only to declared version changes.

title: Phase 4: implement brew_packages and brew_tap with run-as-user support
description: Implement the Homebrew provider family for formulae, casks, and taps while honoring the documented unprivileged-user execution requirement.
design: Keep taps with brew packages because taps are a prerequisite edge inside the same provider ecosystem and share run-as-user command execution.
acceptance: Homebrew reads installed formulae and casks plus current taps, tap resources order before dependent packages, and all brew operations execute as the configured unprivileged user rather than root.

title: Phase 4: implement npm and python package providers with unprivileged execution
description: Implement global npm package management and the pipx-backed Python CLI package provider using the run-as-user contract documented for workstation tooling.
design: Keep npm and Python tooling together because both depend on the same non-root execution semantics and workstation package-management ergonomics.
acceptance: npm_packages reads global packages and emits install operations only for missing tools, python_packages uses pipx-backed state reads and install operations, and both providers run as the configured unprivileged user.

title: Phase 4: implement dnf and pacman package providers
description: Implement the non-Debian package-provider slice for Fedora or RHEL dnf and Arch pacman package management, including the documented AUR-helper support.
design: Separate the alternative distro providers from apt so distro-specific state reads and batching rules stay bounded and independently verifiable.
acceptance: dnf_packages and pacman_packages read installed state, batch missing installs correctly, and pacman supports configured AUR-helper execution for AUR packages.

title: Phase 5: implement user, group, user_in_group, posix_acl, and sudoers_entry providers
description: Implement the user and access-control slice covering users, groups, group membership, POSIX ACLs, and sudoers entries.
design: Keep identity and access-control resources together because they share OS account state reads and are often validated together in workstation and server manifests.
acceptance: User and group resources create missing principals idempotently, membership updates only when needed, ACLs and sudoers entries converge without duplicate rules, and plan output remains readable.

title: Phase 5: implement systemd_service and systemd_unit providers
description: Implement service-state management and inline unit deployment for systemd resources, including ordering between written unit files and service actions.
design: Land systemd resources separately from Docker because unit-file and service-state reads use a different operating-system contract and can be validated in the container harness.
acceptance: systemd_unit writes or updates units idempotently, systemd_service converges enable/start/stop state, and service actions respect dependencies on unit-file changes.

title: Phase 5: implement docker_container provider lifecycle
description: Implement Docker container management covering state reads, stop/remove/run replacement flow, and declared health-check handling.
design: Keep Docker isolated from systemd because container lifecycle semantics and external daemon dependencies create a distinct verification surface.
acceptance: Container state differences produce the documented stop/remove/run cycle, health-check configuration is reflected in the emitted plan, and unchanged containers produce no operations.

title: Phase 5: implement hosts_entry, crypttab_entry, binary_install, and command providers
description: Implement the system-utility provider slice for hosts entries, crypttab rows, binary downloads, and arbitrary command resources used as escape hatches or trigger targets.
design: Group the FEAT-009 providers together because they are utility primitives with straightforward state reads and plan emission patterns that do not require a separate architectural seam.
acceptance: Hosts and crypttab resources converge idempotently, binary installs track declared version markers and extraction steps, and command resources execute only under the documented trigger or explicit-run conditions.

title: Phase 6: implement runtime secret resolution chain and resolve-secrets flow
description: Implement secret references in resource specs, the pre-resolved-file to env to 1Password to custom to fail chain, and the resolve-secrets workflow that runs as the unprivileged user before root apply.
design: Keep runtime secret lookup and pre-resolution together because they are one contract boundary between plan generation, operator workflow, and apply-time value access.
acceptance: Secret references resolve only at execution time, pre-resolved secret files are honored before env or 1Password lookups, optional and missing-secret behavior matches FEAT-001, and custom providers can extend the chain after built-ins.

title: Phase 6: implement secret_file provider and masked-output generation behavior
description: Implement the secret_file provider for writing resolved secrets to disk together with auto-generation fallback and masked rendering in plan and log output.
design: Land secret-file materialization after the runtime chain so provider behavior can focus on file writes, permissions, generation fallback, and operator warnings.
acceptance: secret_file writes resolved or generated values with the declared permissions, generated secrets warn the operator to store the result, and plans or logs never print secret contents.

title: Phase 7: implement zfs_dataset and zfs_properties providers
description: Implement the storage-provider slice for ZFS dataset creation, property management, encryption-related attributes, recursive property updates, and skip-if-missing behavior.
design: Keep both ZFS providers together because they share dataset-state reads, destructive-operation safeguards, and server-only validation semantics.
acceptance: zfs_dataset and zfs_properties emit the correct create or set operations, recursive and skip-if-missing behavior matches FEAT-007, and unchanged dataset state produces no operations.

title: Phase 7: implement kerberos_kdc, kerberos_principal, and kerberos_keytab providers
description: Implement Kerberos realm initialization, idempotent principal creation, and keytab export for the authentication-provider slice.
design: Land the Kerberos providers together because they share kadmin or kdb state reads and are required as one coherent authentication subsystem for the server deployment.
acceptance: kerberos_kdc initializes only when absent, kerberos_principal creation is idempotent, kerberos_keytab export writes the requested principals to disk safely, and plan output distinguishes no-op from create or export operations.

title: Phase 9: migrate the timbuktu reference deployment into governed Anneal manifests
description: Translate the timbuktu server deployment into Anneal manifests with governed provider coverage and explicit gaps for any unsupported resources.
design: Keep the server migration separate from workstation work because timbuktu depends on the storage, authentication, service, secrets, and system-provider stack.
acceptance: The repo contains a governed timbuktu manifest set covering every server capability that is authorized by the artifact stack, and any unsupported scope is tracked explicitly rather than hidden in ad hoc scripts.

title: Phase 9: migrate the sahara reference deployment into governed Anneal manifests
description: Translate the sahara workstation deployment into Anneal manifests covering package, file, user, service, and utility resources with template conversion where needed.
design: Keep the workstation migration separate from the server path so the package and developer-tooling surface can iterate independently.
acceptance: The repo contains a governed sahara manifest set that maps the current workstation provisioning shell scripts into Anneal resources with explicit variable and template usage.

title: Phase 9: add side-by-side validation and migration documentation for reference deployments
description: Add the side-by-side validation workflow and migration documentation required to prove that the migrated reference manifests behave like the existing shell-based deployments.
design: Keep validation and docs together because both are deployment-evidence deliverables over the same migrated manifest sets.
acceptance: The repo documents how to validate Anneal manifests against the current timbuktu and sahara shell workflows, and that validation produces durable evidence for migration readiness.

title: Validation milestone after Phase 1: prove single-file manifest convergence
description: Track the evidence required for the Phase 1 milestone that a single-file manifest can validate, plan, and apply end to end.
design: Bind the milestone to the exact Phase 1 execution slices and the tests that prove single-resource convergence.
acceptance: This issue identifies the test or evidence required to show anneal plan and anneal apply work for a single-file manifest after Phase 1 work closes.

title: Validation milestone after Phase 3: prove multi-file manifest composition
description: Track the evidence required for the Phase 3 milestone that multi-file manifests with includes and iterators plan correctly.
design: Tie the milestone to include, iterator, and trigger slices so the required golden or integration coverage is explicit before implementation starts.
acceptance: This issue identifies the test or evidence required to show multi-file manifests with includes, iterators, and notify/trigger behavior plan correctly after Phase 3 work closes.

title: Validation milestone after Phase 5: prove sahara workstation manifest coverage
description: Track the evidence required for the Phase 5 milestone that the sahara workstation manifest covers packages, users, files, and services.
design: Keep the milestone blocked on the workstation-facing provider slices so missing prerequisites stay visible in the queue.
acceptance: This issue identifies the test or evidence required to prove the sahara workstation manifest covers packages, users, files, and services after the requisite provider slices close.

title: Validation milestone after Phase 7: prove timbuktu server manifest coverage
description: Track the evidence required for the Phase 7 milestone that the timbuktu server manifest covers ZFS, Kerberos, NFS, Samba, and Docker.
design: Keep the milestone explicitly blocked on the unresolved NFS/Samba authority gap so the queue cannot silently claim milestone readiness before the artifact stack is complete.
acceptance: This issue identifies the test or evidence required to prove the timbuktu server manifest covers the full Phase 7 milestone scope, including any newly authorized NFS or Samba work.

title: Validation milestone after Phase 9: prove fresh-install convergence for both reference deployments
description: Track the final evidence required for the Phase 9 milestone that both reference deployments converge from a fresh OS install.
design: Tie the final milestone to the migrated manifests plus the side-by-side validation workflow so convergence proof is explicit in the queue.
acceptance: This issue identifies the test or evidence required to prove both reference deployments converge from fresh installs after the migration work closes.

title: User story ID collision: US-010 in both FEAT-002 and FEAT-003
description: The gap analysis commit renumbered FEAT-002 user stories (US-005→007, US-006→008, US-007→009, US-008→010). US-010 now exists in both FEAT-002 (Trigger a restart on config change) and FEAT-003 (Install system packages). User story IDs must be unique across all features.
acceptance: US IDs are unique across all feature specs. Renumber either FEAT-002 or FEAT-003 stories to eliminate the collision.

title: PRD P0-14 says pip but should say pipx
description: PRD line 130 says 'Required for Homebrew, npm, pip, and other tools that must not run as root.' The gap analysis changed US-017 from pip to pipx (PEP 668 compliance), and FEAT-003/SD-001 both say pipx. The PRD should be consistent.
acceptance: PRD P0-14 references pipx, not pip, matching FEAT-003 US-017 and SD-001.

title: SD-001 traceability table stale after PRD P0 renumbering
description: PRD inserted P0-14 (run-as-user) and P0-17 (template variables), renumbering fail-stop to P0-15 and single binary to P0-16. SD-001 traceability table still maps P0-14→fail-stop and P0-15→single binary. Missing entries for P0-14 (run-as-user) and P0-17 (template variables).
acceptance: SD-001 traceability table matches current PRD numbering. All P0 requirements have traceability entries.

title: US-016 (npm) missing run-as-user acceptance criterion
description: FEAT-003 dependency section states 'brew, npm, pipx must run as unprivileged user.' US-013 (brew) and US-017 (pipx) have explicit run-as-user acceptance criteria, but US-016 (npm) does not.
acceptance: US-016 includes an acceptance criterion that npm operations run as the unprivileged user, not root.

title: FEAT-001 US-005 secret chain order missing pre-resolved position
description: SD-001 specifies secret chain order as: pre-resolved file → env vars → 1Password → custom → fail. FEAT-001 US-005 acceptance criteria still say 'env vars → 1Password' without specifying where pre-resolved secrets fit.
acceptance: FEAT-001 US-005 acceptance criteria document the complete secret provider chain order matching SD-001.

title: Build manifest and provider scenario matrix unit tests
description: Expand the test harness with table-driven unit coverage for manifest composition, variable precedence, secrets, run-as-user, and representative provider state combinations so the core engine and providers are verified against multiple configuration shapes rather than single happy paths.
design: Keep this slice at the unit and golden-test layer: reusable fixtures, table-driven cases, and mock-system coverage for config-loader and provider drift states. It should improve proof density without depending on full provider execution in containers.
acceptance: The repo contains table-driven unit and or golden tests covering multiple manifest and provider combinations, including variable precedence, notify or trigger behavior, secret resolution ordering, run-as-user, and representative provider present or absent or drifted states.

title: Build Docker OS-matrix integration harness for providers and modules
description: Add a Docker-based integration matrix that exercises provider and manifest-composition behavior across supported Linux distribution families and representative configuration combinations.
design: Start with a reusable container harness and fixture runner that can execute end-to-end provider scenarios on Debian or Ubuntu, Fedora-like, and Arch images, with room for Linux Homebrew coverage where feasible. Keep unsupported non-Docker platforms out of scope for this slice.
acceptance: The repo contains a Docker-driven integration harness that can run representative provider or module scenarios on at least Debian or Ubuntu, Fedora-like, and Arch Linux containers, and the harness is wired so additional provider scenarios can be added incrementally.

title: Add reproducible screencast smoke proof for the core workflow
description: Create a reproducible terminal screencast smoke test that proves the documented validate to plan to apply to idempotent re-apply flow on a small but real manifest.
design: Use a repo-owned script and fixtures to record or replay a deterministic terminal session so the smoke proof can run in CI or release verification without depending on manual narration. The scenario should exercise at least one real provider path.
acceptance: The repo contains a reproducible screencast smoke fixture and script that demonstrate validate, plan, apply, and idempotent re-apply on a representative manifest, and the smoke run can be invoked as part of automated verification.

title: Fix heredoc delimiter injection in file provider plan output
description: engine.go:106 uses a fixed ANNEAL_EOF heredoc delimiter. If file content contains that literal string on its own line, the heredoc terminates early and remaining content is executed as shell commands.
acceptance: stdlib_file_write heredoc uses a delimiter that cannot appear in the content; test proves content containing the delimiter does not cause injection

title: Shell-quote path, mode, and owner in file provider plan output
description: engine.go:106 interpolates path, mode, and owner directly into a shell command without quoting. Paths with spaces or metacharacters break the script or allow injection.
acceptance: File paths with spaces and shell metacharacters produce correct plan output; test proves quoting is applied

title: Env override collision risk: manifest vars matching system env names
description: lookupEnvOverride (manifest.go:207-222) checks raw, uppercase, and underscore-normalized forms of manifest var keys against the environment. A manifest var named path silently picks up the system PATH.
acceptance: Either env overrides are namespaced (e.g. ANNEAL_ prefix) or the collision risk is documented with a test demonstrating the behavior

title: Builtins.withDefaults() overwrites caller-supplied Hostname for FQDN fallback
description: manifest.go:186-190: when b.FQDN is empty, code sets b.FQDN = b.Hostname then overwrites with current.FQDN (os.Hostname). If caller sets Hostname=custom but leaves FQDN empty, FQDN becomes the real system hostname.
acceptance: When Builtins.Hostname is set but FQDN is empty, FQDN defaults to the caller-supplied Hostname, not the system hostname

title: Shell generation safety patterns for plan output
description: Learnings from fresh-eyes review of commit 32e2e29: When generating shell scripts in plan output, all providers must (1) use dynamic heredoc delimiters verified not to appear in content, (2) shell-quote all interpolated values, and (3) treat plan output as an attack surface since manifests may contain user-controlled strings.
acceptance: N/A - knowledge capture

title: template_file provider lacks spec-required validate command support
description: FEAT-005 US-031 requires template_file to support an optional validate command that runs against rendered content before the plan reports the change as valid. The implementation only renders and compares content; it never reads or executes spec.validate, so invalid rendered configs are accepted into the plan.
design: Add template_file validate handling that runs the configured command against rendered content at plan time and suppresses plan output when validation fails.
acceptance: template_file accepts an optional validate command, plan fails with the validation error when the rendered content is invalid, and tests cover both passing and failing validation commands.

title: file_copy saved plans do not detect source-file drift
description: file_copy emits stdlib_file_copy with only the source path. The provider reads source content during planning to decide whether to emit, but that content is not reflected in the saved plan text. If the source file changes after plan generation, re-plan emits the same script and saved-plan drift detection cannot detect the change, so apply may execute different bytes than were reviewed.
design: Make file_copy plans content-addressed: either embed the source content in the plan, or emit and verify a source checksum so re-plan output changes whenever source bytes change.
acceptance: Changing a file_copy source file after saving a plan causes apply --plan to abort on drift before execution. Tests cover saved-plan drift detection for file_copy source mutations.

title: file providers ignore mode and owner drift when content is already converged
description: template_file, static_file, and file_copy all return no operations as soon as destination content matches the desired bytes. They do not inspect file mode or ownership first, so metadata drift is left uncorrected. FEAT-005 requires mode/owner enforcement for managed files, and file_copy explicitly requires destination mode enforcement.
design: Read destination stat metadata alongside content and emit chmod/chown work when metadata differs, even when content is unchanged. Add tests covering content-equal but mode/owner-drifted files.
acceptance: Managed file providers converge mode and owner independently of content equality. Tests prove chmod/chown operations are emitted when content matches but metadata differs.

title: Add golden-file plan tests (Tier 2)
description: The test plan defines Tier 2 golden-file tests but none exist. Plan output regressions are invisible without byte-for-byte comparison of known-input-to-expected-output. Create testdata/ with representative manifests and .golden plan files covering: single file resource, multi-resource DAG ordering, converged no-op, template rendering, custom mode/owner, heredoc delimiter edge case.
design: Create testdata/golden/ with .yaml manifest and .golden expected plan output pairs. Add a TestGoldenFiles table-driven test that loads each pair, runs BuildPlan, and compares Script() output byte-for-byte. Include an -update flag for regenerating golden files.
acceptance: testdata/golden/ contains at least 6 manifest/golden pairs. A single table-driven test loads all pairs and fails on any diff. go test -run TestGolden -update regenerates the golden files.

title: Add direct EmbeddedShell and stdlib integration tests
description: EmbeddedShell and stdlib preamble functions have zero direct tests. They are only exercised indirectly through CLI apply. A malformed stdlib function would pass all provider unit tests (which use MockSystem) and only fail at runtime.
design: Create engine/stdlib_test.go with tests that execute each stdlib function through EmbeddedShell against temp directories. Verify file creation, content, mode, parent directory creation, and error cases.
acceptance: Every stdlib shell function has at least one direct test executing through EmbeddedShell. Tests verify filesystem effects. Malformed stdlib would be caught without CLI-level tests.

title: Fix incomplete saved-plan drift detection test
description: TestApplyWithSavedPlanDriftDetection in cli_test.go is incomplete — saves a plan and applies with matching state, but the drift scenario is commented out. This is a P0 requirement with a hole in coverage.
design: Complete the test: after saving the plan, mutate the target file so re-plan produces different output, then verify apply --plan aborts with a drift error before execution.
acceptance: TestApplyWithSavedPlanDriftDetection covers both matching and drift cases. Drift case proves apply aborts before execution when state changed after plan generation.

title: Add manifest parser fuzz testing
description: The manifest parser combines YAML decoding, Go template evaluation with Sprig, and variable resolution — a rich attack surface with no fuzz tests. Fuzzing would catch panics, infinite loops, and unexpected error paths.
design: Add FuzzLoadManifest and FuzzRenderString fuzz targets using Go native fuzzing. Seed corpus with valid manifests, edge cases, and known-bad inputs.
acceptance: go test -fuzz FuzzLoadManifest and FuzzRenderString run without panics on seed corpus. Both have seed corpora with at least 10 entries.

title: Add mvdan/sh interpreter conformance tests
description: Test plan requires interpreter conformance tests for mvdan/sh covering shell features used by providers: heredocs, command substitution, pipes, conditionals, functions, quoting. None exist. A mvdan/sh upgrade could change behavior with no regression net.
design: Create engine/interp_test.go with table-driven tests executing shell snippets through EmbeddedShell and asserting expected output or exit status. Cover at least 10 shell feature scenarios.
acceptance: Table-driven interpreter conformance suite covers at least 10 shell feature scenarios. Tests run through EmbeddedShell and verify output/exit behavior.
